collections.title: Falling Seven Times, Rising Eight
collections.blurb: Japanese sayings about resilience, patient endurance and the courage to begin again.

proverbs.translation: Fall seven times, stand up eight.
proverbs.meaning: Real strength is not in never falling but in always getting back up.

proverbs.translation: Enduring with patience and dignity.
proverbs.meaning: Bearing hardship quietly, without losing your composure or complaining.

proverbs.translation: Do your best — keep going.
proverbs.meaning: A spirited cheer of encouragement: persevere, give it everything you have.

paragraphs: Few cultures celebrate the art of getting back up like Japan. The proverb nana korobi ya oki teaches that a truly resilient spirit is measured not by how rarely you fall, but by how persistent you are in rising again. Even the most determined hero faltered before they ever triumphed.

Gaman is the quiet sister of this courage. It is the resolute, vigorous patience that lets a person carry on through cold winters, long study nights and difficult exams without complaint. To practise gaman is to keep your back straight even when your legs have staggered.

Children in Japanese classrooms are sent off to tests with the cheerful word ganbatte, a valiant little battle cry meaning give it your best. It is the reminder that effort itself is something to be proud of, whatever the score on the page.

Together these three sayings paint a picture of a diligent, unhurried strength. Setbacks are not endings but invitations. Tumble, breathe, and stand once more — and you will, in the end, walk away triumphant.

collections.title: Drop by Drop, the Stone Wears Through
collections.blurb: Chinese sayings on patience, small steps and the quiet power of persistence.

proverbs.romanisation: Qiānlǐ zhī xíng, shǐ yú zúxià
proverbs.translation: A journey of a thousand miles begins beneath one's feet.
proverbs.meaning: Even the most arduous goal starts with the same first step you can take right now. (Laozi)

proverbs.translation: Dripping water hollows out stone.
proverbs.meaning: Soft, persistent effort beats sudden, violent effort every time.

proverbs.translation: An iron rod can be ground down into a needle.
proverbs.meaning: With enough diligent grinding, even the hardest task can be finished.

paragraphs: Some of the oldest Chinese wisdom centres on a simple truth: greatness is not a leap, but a long line of small, determined footsteps. Laozi's famous line that a thousand-mile journey begins beneath your feet has comforted travellers, students and statesmen for over two thousand years.
However arduous the road, the first stride is the same size for everyone. There is no need to picture the distant apex of the mountain; there is only the next stone on the path.

Water teaches the same lesson in a softer way. A single drop is harmless, but ceaseless, successive drops can hollow out solid rock. Persistent effort, repeated quietly, can carve through what brute force cannot.

The story of the iron pestle ground into a sewing needle reaches even further. A boy bored of his studies meets an old woman patiently rubbing an iron rod against a stone, and learns that no diligent task is impossible if you refuse to stop.
Whether you feel stuck on a plateau or are only one resolute step from the summit, these proverbs all whisper the same encouragement: keep going, and you will arrive triumphant.

collections.title: Three Walking Together, One a Teacher
collections.blurb: Confucian sayings on humble learning, honest knowing and the golden rule.

proverbs.romanisation: Xué ér bù sī zé wǎng
proverbs.translation: Learning without thinking is wasted.
proverbs.meaning: Facts you do not contemplate will slip straight through your mind. (Confucius)

proverbs.romanisation: Sān rén xíng, bì yǒu wǒ shī
proverbs.translation: When three people walk together, one must be my teacher.
proverbs.meaning: Every person you meet can teach you something — if you are tolerant enough to listen. (Confucius)

proverbs.native: 知之為知之，不知為不知
proverbs.romanisation: Zhī zhī wéi zhī zhī, bù zhī wéi bù zhī
proverbs.translation: Know what you know; admit what you don't.
proverbs.meaning: True wisdom begins the moment you acknowledge the edges of your own understanding. (Confucius)

proverbs.romanisation: Jǐ suǒ bù yù, wù shī yú rén
proverbs.translation: Do not do to others what you would not want done to you.
proverbs.meaning: The Confucian golden rule — measure your actions by the compassion you would want returned.

paragraphs: Confucius believed that learning was less about stuffing facts into your head and more about training the mind to think. To read a page and then not contemplate it, he said, is to learn nothing at all. A rational mind chews its food.

He was also deeply humble about how knowledge grows. When three people walk together, he taught, one of them is certainly your teacher — perhaps in what to do, perhaps in what to avoid. A tolerant student keeps their ears open even to those they might be tempted to dismiss.

His most piercing lesson is about the limits of our own insight. To pretend you know what you don't is foolish; to acknowledge the gap is the first real step toward wisdom. Treat every confident hypothesis as a question, not a conclusion.

Finally, long before such ideas became a Western convention, Confucius gave the world its golden rule: never do to others what you would hate done to yourself. It is a small, eager habit of compassion that quietly corrects our most subjective impulses.

collections.title: The Old Man Who Lost His Horse
collections.blurb: Sayings that teach calm foresight in a turning, unpredictable world.

proverbs.romanisation: Sài wēng shī mǎ, yān zhī fēi fú
proverbs.translation: When the old man lost his horse, how could anyone know it was not a blessing?
proverbs.meaning: Today's misfortune may be tomorrow's good luck — judge no single moment too quickly.

proverbs.translation: In times of peace, think of danger.
proverbs.meaning: Stay vigilant even when life feels calm; the wise plan in good weather for the storm.

proverbs.translation: Prevent trouble before it sprouts.
proverbs.meaning: The cautious traveller mends the roof while the sun shines.

paragraphs: In an old Chinese tale, a farmer loses his horse. His neighbours moan that it is a calamity. The old man only shrugs. The next week the horse returns with a wild companion — but soon after, his son falls from the new horse and breaks his leg. Misfortune again? Perhaps. Then an army comes to take young men to war, and the injured son is left at home. Each circumstance turned out to be the opposite of what it seemed.

The proverb 'when the old man lost his horse, how can anyone know it was not a blessing?' became a calm reminder that a single moment is never the whole story. Volatile events look very different from the safe distance of next year.

Two related sayings urge sensible foresight. 'In times of peace, think of danger' tells us to remain vigilant when everything looks easy, because a perilous storm is always looming somewhere over the horizon.

'Prevent trouble before it sprouts' is the warily practical cousin of the same idea. A cautious gardener pulls the weed while it is still small; a cautious king plans for the lean year while the harvest is still rich. Anticipation, not panic, is what keeps a precarious situation from becoming a disaster.

collections.title: Know Yourself, Know Your Enemy
collections.blurb: Sun Tzu's lessons on preparation, self-knowledge and why the smartest fights are the ones you never have to finish.

proverbs.romanisation: Zhī jǐ zhī bǐ, bǎi zhàn bù dài
proverbs.translation: Know yourself and know your enemy; in a hundred battles you will not be imperilled.
proverbs.meaning: Knowing both your own weaknesses and your opponent's strengths is what separates strategy from guesswork. (Sun Tzu)

proverbs.translation: War is the art of deception.
proverbs.meaning: Competitions are rarely won by whoever tries hardest. They are won by whoever thinks most clearly and prepares most thoroughly. (Sun Tzu)

proverbs.translation: The best strategy attacks the enemy's plans.
proverbs.meaning: If you can see what someone intends to do before they do it, you have already won half the contest. (Sun Tzu)

paragraphs: Two and a half thousand years ago, a Chinese general named Sun Tzu wrote a short book still read by military commanders, chess players, sports coaches and business leaders today. The Art of War begins not with courage or physical strength but with knowledge. Understanding the situation clearly — including your own blind spots — is the most decisive advantage anyone can have.

Know yourself and know your enemy, and in a hundred battles you will not be imperilled. This is a determined challenge. It asks: how well do you actually know your own weaknesses? Your own patterns under pressure? And how carefully do you scrutinise the skills of whoever you are competing against? Most people do one of these things. The truly resolute competitor does both.

Sun Tzu also says: war is deception. By war he means any serious contest. The scouts who watch from a distance, the general whose espionage gives them information before the battle begins — these are the ones who win. Insight gathered early, through cautious vigilance, is worth far more than ten times the effort once the contest has already started.

The third principle is the most elegant: the best strategy attacks the enemy's plans. If your anticipation and perception are good enough to see what someone intends before they do it, you can remove the threat before it ever fully arrives. This is the opposite of reacting — it is acting early, from knowledge.

Children who learn this way of thinking are not being taught to be dishonest. They are being taught to be observant and prepared. A team that knows how the opposing side plays and has a quiet plan ready has a real advantage — even before a ball has been kicked.

collections.title: Appear Weak When You Are Strong
collections.blurb: Sun Tzu's counter-intuitive lessons on concealment, timing and why the greatest victories are the ones you never had to fight for.

proverbs.romanisation: Néng ér shì zhī bù néng
proverbs.translation: When able, appear unable.
proverbs.meaning: The person who reveals their real strengths is giving away information. A skilled competitor looks weaker than they are to draw out a careless opponent. (Sun Tzu)

proverbs.native: 不戰而屈人之兵，善之善者也
proverbs.romanisation: Bù zhàn ér qū rén zhī bīng, shàn zhī shàn zhě yě
proverbs.translation: To subdue the enemy without fighting is the greatest skill.
proverbs.meaning: The best outcome is the one where your position was so obviously strong that the contest never needed to happen. (Sun Tzu)

proverbs.romanisation: Bīng guì shèng, bù guì jiǔ
proverbs.translation: In any contest, what matters is winning; what does not matter is how long it lasts.
proverbs.meaning: A quick, decisive result is almost always better than a long, draining struggle. Save your strength for what comes next. (Sun Tzu)

paragraphs: Sun Tzu was fascinated by appearances — specifically the gap between how things look and how they actually are. His most counter-intuitive instruction is this: when you are strong, appear weak. When you are ready, appear hesitant. The goal is to keep your real position concealed for as long as possible, so the opponent commits before they know what they are facing.

This is not dishonesty for its own sake. An opponent who believes you are struggling will become careless. They will over-commit too early, revealing their own clandestine plans while yours remain shrouded. The stealthy, patient competitor who lets others underestimate them is using this principle every time. The result looks like a surprise — but it was planned.

To subdue the enemy without fighting is the best skill of all. This disturbed some of Sun Tzu's original readers. Was the general suggesting cowardice? No — something more subtle and ingenious: a position so obviously strong that the opponent decided not to fight. The victory that never needed to happen is the most efficient victory of all.

The third principle is swift and practical: win quickly. A long contest drains both sides. The conviction to act decisively at the right moment, after careful preparation, is worth far more than endless evasive back-and-forth. When the moment comes, act with speed and purpose.

Children use this well at its best. The debater who looks uncertain at the start and then delivers a well-prepared conclusion. The sports team that hides its best tactic until it counts. These are not tricks — they are strategy. Looking weak when strong is a skill that takes patience and practice to develop.

collections.title: Rewards and Punishments Must Be Clear
collections.blurb: Han Feizi's unsentimental lessons on why consistent rules applied equally to everyone are what actually hold any group together.

proverbs.translation: Rewards and punishments must be clearly divided.
proverbs.meaning: Any group works best when the rules are the same for everyone and the results of following or breaking them are entirely predictable.

proverbs.translation: The law does not flatter the noble.
proverbs.meaning: Rules that bend for the powerful are not rules — they are decoration. Real order requires that nobody is above the system. (Han Feizi)

proverbs.translation: Trustworthy rewards, certain punishments.
proverbs.meaning: People follow rules consistently only when they believe the consequences are real — for good work and for bad.

paragraphs: Around 300 BC, a Chinese thinker named Han Feizi wrote one of the most unsentimental books about leadership ever produced. He was not interested in whether rulers were kind or virtuous. He was interested in what actually made a society stable. His answer was simple: clear rules, applied to everyone, with reliable consequences.

Rewards and punishments must be clearly divided. This sounds obvious — but Han Feizi was pointing at something every group tends to get wrong. Allocating rewards to friends while directing rebuke only at enemies destroys trust from within. If people cannot predict what will happen when they follow or break the rules, they stop believing in those rules at all.

The law does not flatter the noble. In a society built on strict hierarchy, this was a radical idea. Han Feizi argued that a lawful system must compel the same standard from a powerful minister as from an ordinary person. The most important person in the hierarchy should face the same deterrent consequence for breaking a rule as the least important one. Anything else is not law — it is just power.

Trustworthy rewards, certain punishments. People will not follow a convention they do not believe in. If good work goes without acknowledgement, good work stops. If bad behaviour is regularly excused, it spreads. Consistency is not cruelty — it is the only thing that makes any mandate fair enough to be worth following.

These ideas reach far beyond ancient China. Every classroom, every sports team and every family runs into Han Feizi's principles eventually. The rules do not have to be strict to be fair. They just have to be real, and they have to discriminate between good and poor behaviour the same way every single time.

collections.title: Stones Rolling Down the Mountain
collections.blurb: Sun Tzu on momentum, positioning and why where you stand before the contest begins is often more important than how hard you fight.

proverbs.native: 激水之疾，至於漂石者，勢也
proverbs.romanisation: Jī shuǐ zhī jí, zhì yú piāo shí zhě, shì yě
proverbs.translation: The speed of rushing water that carries boulders — this is momentum.
proverbs.meaning: At the right moment, even something small and fast can move what seems immovable. Position and timing create force that raw effort alone cannot. (Sun Tzu)

proverbs.romanisation: Qiú zhī yú shì, bù zé yú rén
proverbs.translation: Seek victory in positioning, not in blame on individuals.
proverbs.meaning: If a team keeps failing, the problem is usually how things are set up — not which single person is at fault. Fix the structure. (Sun Tzu)

proverbs.native: 故善戰者，如轉圓石於千仞之山者
proverbs.romanisation: Gù shàn zhàn zhě, rú zhuǎn yuán shí yú qiān rèn zhī shān zhě
proverbs.translation: The skilled commander is like rolling round boulders from a towering mountain.
proverbs.meaning: Build your position patiently over time, and when you release it, the force is unstoppable. (Sun Tzu)

paragraphs: Sun Tzu was fascinated by a force he called shì — momentum or strategic advantage. The idea is that at the right moment, a well-positioned force can achieve far more than a stronger force at the wrong moment. He compared it to water: a trickle is harmless, but water moving fast enough from the right height can carry boulders downstream. The power comes not from the water itself but from the position it fell from.

The soldier who surged forward at the perfect moment, from a towering position of advantage, with surprise behind them, was not necessarily stronger than their opponent. They were in a better place at a better time, after more patient preparation. The looming advantage was built long before the contest began.

Seek victory in positioning, not in blame on individuals. If a team keeps losing or a project keeps lurching sideways, the reflex is to find someone to rebuke. Sun Tzu's persistent advice was different: look at the structure. Is everyone in the right place? A good position makes ordinary people effective. A bad one defeats even the most determined talent.

Every goal reached through successive small steps demonstrates this principle. The student who reaches the apex of a difficult exam does not vault there in a single night — they build to it steadily, from a plateau of daily preparation. By the time the test arrives, they have already climbed most of the mountain.

The lesson for children is simple: before forcing your way through a hard problem, ask whether you are in the right position. Have you built enough preparation? The patient, well-positioned approach is not slow. It is the one that rolls boulders.

collections.title: When the Lips Are Gone, the Teeth Grow Cold
collections.blurb: Warring States wisdom on alliances, geography and the art of thinking several moves ahead before agreeing to anything.

proverbs.translation: When the lips are gone, the teeth grow cold.
proverbs.meaning: Neighbours who protect each other stay safe. When one is destroyed, the other is next. (Zuo Zhuan)

proverbs.translation: Befriend the distant; deal carefully with the near.
proverbs.meaning: An ally far away cannot easily threaten you. A powerful neighbour in close proximity almost always can. (Warring States strategy)

proverbs.translation: Borrow a road to attack Guo.
proverbs.meaning: Beware the small favour requested by someone with larger plans. Letting a powerful force through your door once may mean you cannot close it again. (36 Stratagems)

paragraphs: During China's turbulent Warring States period, rulers of small kingdoms had to make constant, life-or-death decisions about who to side with and who to be wary of. From those centuries of hard calculation came some of the sharpest strategic proverbs in history — not about fighting bravely, but about thinking carefully before anyone drew a sword.

When the lips are gone, the teeth grow cold. This proverb comes from a story in which one small kingdom allowed a belligerent neighbour to march through its domestic territory to attack another realm. The warning was ignored. When the hostile army finished destroying the second kingdom, it turned around and took the first one too. Being vigilant about what you agree to — and who benefits from your cooperation — is not paranoia. It is basic self-preservation.

Befriend the distant; deal carefully with the near. A powerful ally on the other side of the world cannot easily project force into your daily affairs. A powerful neighbour in close proximity can, and eventually the temptation to do so grows. The lesson is not about personal friendship — it is about the realistic geometry of collective security.

The third proverb is the craftiest. In the original story, a powerful kingdom needed to cross a small kingdom's land to attack an enemy. They offered generous gifts and allege friendship. The small king accepted. The army marched through — then turned around and took the small kingdom on the return journey. A deterrent position, once given away for a small favour, is very difficult to recover.

For children, these proverbs are about thinking several steps ahead before agreeing to something. When someone asks for something small, a vigilant mind asks: what is the bigger picture here? Who benefits from my yes? The habit of watching for patterns — not just reacting to the immediate moment — is one of the most useful habits anyone can build.

collections.title: Know Thyself
collections.blurb: Greek sayings on self-knowledge — the famous starting line of philosophy.

proverbs.translation: Know thyself.
proverbs.meaning: Carved above the Temple of Apollo at Delphi — the first task of any wise person.

proverbs.translation: I know one thing — that I know nothing.
proverbs.meaning: The honest starting point for real learning, attributed to Socrates.

proverbs.native: ὁ ἀνεξέταστος βίος οὐ βιωτὸς ἀνθρώπῳ
proverbs.romanisation: Ho anexétastos bíos ou biōtós anthrṓpōi
proverbs.translation: The unexamined life is not worth living.
proverbs.meaning: A life you never stop to scrutinise is barely a life at all. (Socrates)

paragraphs: Above the entrance to the Temple of Apollo at Delphi, two solemn Greek words were carved: Gnōthi seautón — know thyself. Pilgrims came from across the ancient world for answers, only to be reminded at the door that the most important question was about the person now asking.

Socrates carried this further. He wandered the streets of Athens insisting that he knew only one thing — that he knew nothing. It is the line that founded Western philosophy: a calm acknowledgement that real insight begins the moment we drop the delusion of already understanding.

He pressed his fellow citizens to contemplate their lives, not just live them. 'The unexamined life,' he warned, 'is not worth living.' To never pause and scrutinise your own perception, your own bias, your own subjective certainty is, he said, to sleepwalk through your own days.

These three sayings together form a quiet sequence: notice yourself, acknowledge what you do not know, and turn that honesty into conviction. It is hard, lifelong work — and the start of every other kind of wisdom.

collections.title: Nothing in Excess
collections.blurb: Greek wisdom on balance, moderation and the danger of too much of anything.

proverbs.translation: Nothing in excess.
proverbs.meaning: The second great Delphic motto: a temperate life is a wise life.

proverbs.translation: Moderation is best.
proverbs.meaning: Attributed to Cleobulus — the right measure beats the most lavish quantity.

proverbs.translation: Excellence; virtue.
proverbs.meaning: For Aristotle, every virtue sits as a 'golden mean' between two extremes — courage between cowardice and recklessness, generosity between meanness and waste.

paragraphs: Beside 'Know thyself' at the Temple of Apollo stood a second Greek motto: Mēdèn ágan — nothing in excess. The Greeks were suspicious of every voracious appetite. Too little food was misery, but too much was a different kind of trap. The temperate path, in their eyes, was the wise one.

Cleobulus, one of the Seven Sages, put it even more simply: métron áriston, 'moderation is best.' A thrifty supper with friends, they thought, would feed the soul more richly than a lavish, opulent banquet eaten alone. Quality of measure beat quantity of stuff.

Aristotle gave this idea its most subtle shape. Every virtuous quality, he argued, sits between two faults. Courage is the calm point between cowardice and reckless, extravagant daring. Generosity sits between stinginess and showing off. To find this golden mean takes rigour, attention and a steady eye.

The Greek invitation is gentle but demanding: enough, but not too much. A sufficient slice of cake, a fair share of praise, a reasonable bedtime. Excess in any direction, they noticed, tends to break the very thing we love.

collections.title: You Cannot Step in the Same River Twice
collections.blurb: Greek sayings on change, fate and the rivers that never stop flowing.

proverbs.translation: Everything flows.
proverbs.meaning: Nothing stays still — the world is in constant, rippling change. (Heraclitus)

proverbs.native: δὶς ἐς τὸν αὐτὸν ποταμὸν οὐκ ἂν ἐμβαίης
proverbs.romanisation: Dìs es tòn autòn potamòn ouk àn embaíēs
proverbs.translation: You cannot step into the same river twice.
proverbs.meaning: The river has moved on. So have you. (Heraclitus)

proverbs.translation: Along with Athena, move your hand too.
proverbs.meaning: Pray to the goddess of wisdom if you like — but also lift your own hand and do the work.

paragraphs: Heraclitus stood by a river and said the most famous thing any Greek ever said about change: pánta rheî — 'everything flows.' Rocks look solid, mountains seem immortal, friendships feel fixed; but in truth, every successive moment is a little different from the one before.

His other line drives the point home. You cannot step into the same river twice. The water that touched your foot a second ago has already surged downstream. The variant, rippling current you meet now is brand new, and so, in tiny ways, are you. Life is more ephemeral than it looks.

This sounds gloomy, but the Greeks turned it into a calm courage. If the world is volatile and turbulent — sometimes inconsistent, sometimes faded into twilight — then anchoring our hopes in 'things never changing' is foolish. The wise person learns to walk with the flow.

A third proverb reminds us we are not just passengers in this current. 'Along with Athena, move your hand too' — pray, hope, dream if you wish, but also pick up the oar and pull. Fate likes a person who is rowing.

collections.title: Seize the Day
collections.blurb: Latin sayings on the shortness of life and the urgency of living it well.

proverbs.translation: Seize the day.
proverbs.meaning: Don't postpone living to a tomorrow that is not promised. (Horace, Odes 1.11)

proverbs.translation: Remember that you must die.
proverbs.meaning: Roman generals were whispered this in triumphal parades — a reminder that all earthly glory ends.

proverbs.translation: Time flies.
proverbs.meaning: The hours slip by quicker than you expect. Use them.

proverbs.translation: Thus passes the glory of the world.
proverbs.meaning: Even the most glorious empire fades. Live for what truly lasts.

paragraphs: When the Roman poet Horace wrote carpe diem — 'seize the day' — he was not telling his friend to throw a wild party. He was warning her that life is finite, ephemeral, and dishearteningly short, so the next opportune moment to live well is the one she is in right now.

Generals returning to Rome in triumphant parades had a slave whispering memento mori in their ear — 'remember you will die.' Even at the very apex of glorious victory, they were reminded that they were still earthly, mortal and small. Pride had a leash on it.

Tempus fugit, 'time flies,' is the gentle cousin of the same lesson. The afternoon that felt endless when you were five has already faded into a wistful memory; the school year you have anticipation about will be withered to a single photograph before you know it.

Sic transit gloria mundi closes the thought with a solemn bow: thus passes the glory of the world. The Romans were not telling us to despair, but to choose carefully what we spend our short, eager lives chasing. Some flowers wilt the day they bloom; some, planted well, become orchards.

collections.title: Fortune Favours the Bold
collections.blurb: Latin sayings on courage, hardship and the prize at the end of a hard road.

proverbs.translation: Fortune favours the brave.
proverbs.meaning: Luck has a habit of finding people who already moved their feet. (Virgil, via Pliny)

proverbs.translation: Through hardships to the stars.
proverbs.meaning: The road to anything worth reaching is steep and rough.

proverbs.translation: Love conquers all things.
proverbs.meaning: The bond between people is the most powerful force the Romans knew. (Virgil)

paragraphs: Pliny the Elder is said to have shouted fortes Fortuna iuvat — 'fortune favours the brave' — as he sailed his ship toward the perilous eruption of Mount Vesuvius. The line caught on. The Romans were certain that good luck has a habit of finding people who have already been valiant enough to step forward.

This is not a licence to be reckless. The bravery they admired was a resolute, spirited willingness to face hard things — not a thoughtless dash into them. A courageous person looks at the danger clearly, then moves anyway.

Per aspera ad astra, 'through hardships to the stars,' became one of the favourite mottos of explorers, schools and even space agencies. It is a quiet promise that the road to anything truly worth reaching will be arduous, and that the climb is part of the prize. No one ever surged to the summit on a flat path.

Finally, amor vincit omnia — 'love conquers all.' For all their zealous talk of armies and emperors, the Romans believed in the end that the strongest force in the world was the bond between people. It is what makes the hard road bearable, and the triumphant arrival worth sharing.

collections.title: Make Haste Slowly
collections.blurb: Latin sayings about words, deeds and the wisdom of careful action.

proverbs.translation: I came, I saw, I conquered.
proverbs.meaning: Julius Caesar's brisk, swift report after a lightning victory in 47 BC.

proverbs.translation: Make haste slowly.
proverbs.meaning: Move with urgency, but never carelessly. The motto of Emperor Augustus.

proverbs.translation: Hear the other side.
proverbs.meaning: A fair judge listens patiently to both stories before deciding. A principle still used in modern courts.

proverbs.translation: Deeds, not words.
proverbs.meaning: What you actually do matters far more than what you promise.

paragraphs: Few sentences in history have travelled further than Julius Caesar's three-word boast: vēnī, vīdī, vīcī — 'I came, I saw, I conquered.' Written after a brisk, swift campaign in 47 BC, it has been quoted ever since to celebrate any sudden, determined victory.

Yet Caesar's adopted son Augustus chose almost the opposite motto: festina lente — 'make haste slowly.' Move with energy, he said, but not in panic. Real speed comes from cautious preparation, not from frantic running. The line was so loved that Renaissance printers stamped it on their books.

Audi alteram partem — 'hear the other side' — is a phrase still quoted in modern courts of law. Before you rebuke anyone or harden into a bias, the Roman jurists insisted, you must be tolerant enough to acknowledge the other person's version of events. Half a story is worse than no story.

And acta non verba — 'deeds, not words.' It is easy to declare a noble conviction in front of a cheering crowd. The harder, quieter test is whether your actions on a wet Tuesday morning, when nobody is watching, match the brave things you said on Sunday.

collections.title: Money Has No Smell
collections.blurb: Roman proverbs on wealth, resource management and the honest truth that money matters — however uncomfortable that is to say out loud.

proverbs.translation: Money does not smell.
proverbs.meaning: Emperor Vespasian's reply when his son objected to a tax on public toilets. Money does not carry the history of how it was earned — it is simply money.

proverbs.native: Lucri bonus est odor ex re qualibet
proverbs.romanisation: Lucri bonus est odor ex re qualibet
proverbs.translation: The smell of profit is good from any source.
proverbs.meaning: Juvenal's cutting observation that those who claim to be above money usually have their own private exceptions. (Juvenal, Satires)

proverbs.translation: Everything in Rome has a price.
proverbs.meaning: In any large system — a city, a school, an institution — most things can be negotiated if you find the right currency: attention, effort, access, or money. (Juvenal)

paragraphs: In ancient Rome, the emperor Vespasian introduced a tax on the city's public toilets. When his son complained that this was undignified, Vespasian held up one of the first coins and asked: does this smell? His son admitted it did not. Pecunia non olet — money has no smell. The line has lived for two thousand years because it pointed at something true that polite people prefer to leave unsaid.

What Vespasian identified is a practical reality: money separates from its history once it changes hands. The lavish meal on the table does not announce the debt taken on to furnish it. The opulent gift does not display what was sacrificed to afford it. What matters economically is not how the money was earned but how wisely it is now allocated.

The Roman poet Juvenal pressed the same observation with sharp humour: the smell of profit is good from any source. He was mocking wealthy Romans who lectured about virtue while quietly accepting profitable arrangements they would never mention in public. His point was not that all money is equal — it was that claiming otherwise while still taking it is a kind of performance.

Everything in Rome has a price is not a complaint but a map. In a large, complex hierarchy, resources, access and outcomes can almost always be influenced by finding the right currency. Sometimes that currency is money. Sometimes it is time, attention, loyalty or skill. A thrifty, clear-eyed person who understands this can compensate for many disadvantages.

For children, the lesson is not to become mercenary. It is to develop an honest, grown-up literacy about how resources work. A sufficient understanding of money — how it flows, what it opens, what it cannot buy — is not cynical. Pretending resources do not matter is a luxury that most people cannot afford.

collections.title: If You Want Peace, Prepare for War
collections.blurb: Roman military wisdom on deterrence, preparation and the uncomfortable truth that strength is what actually keeps the peace.

proverbs.translation: If you want peace, prepare for war.
proverbs.meaning: A well-prepared force deters attacks. Peace is not maintained by wishing for goodwill but by making conflict too costly to start. (Vegetius)

proverbs.translation: I will find a way or make one.
proverbs.meaning: Attributed to Hannibal crossing the Alps. An obstacle is not a final answer — it is a problem waiting for someone resolute enough to solve it.

proverbs.translation: Danger is never overcome without danger.
proverbs.meaning: Some problems can only be solved by entering the uncomfortable situation directly. There is no completely safe approach to difficult things. (Publilius Syrus)

paragraphs: Around 400 AD, a Roman military writer named Vegetius collected the lessons of Rome's centuries of warfare into a single manual. His most striking advice was also the simplest: si vis pacem, para bellum — if you want peace, prepare for war. The logic is honest: nobody willingly attacks a robust, well-prepared opponent. Peace is not maintained by hoping for goodwill. It is maintained by making conflict too costly to start.

The Romans built the famous Pax Romana — a long period of relative peace across an enormous empire — on exactly this principle. Legions trained continuously even in peacetime. Fortifications were maintained. The deterrent of a vigilant, determined army meant that many potential conflicts never happened at all. The preparation itself was the peace.

A valiant person who is genuinely prepared rarely has to fight, because opponents calculate their chances first. Cautious, stout preparation builds real confidence — not aggression — and a reputation for being ready is itself a powerful deterrent. You do not need to threaten anyone. You need only to be obviously capable.

I will find a way or make one. When Hannibal's army faced the Alps with no known pass, the response was not to turn back. The resolute conviction that obstacles are problems to be solved — not instructions to stop — is the spirit behind Rome's greatest engineering, its roads, its aqueducts, its bridges.

For children, this is about preparation rather than aggression. You do not avoid difficulty by staying small and quiet. You avoid it by building real capability, and the anticipation to think ahead. Preparation is the quietest form of strength — and usually the most effective.

collections.title: Agreements Must Be Kept
collections.blurb: The Roman law principles that still run through every modern contract, courtroom and promise — and why keeping your word is the foundation of every functioning relationship.

proverbs.translation: Agreements must be kept.
proverbs.meaning: The bedrock of Roman contract law, still used in international treaties today. The whole system of trust rests on promises being honoured.

proverbs.translation: The law is harsh, but it is the law.
proverbs.meaning: Consistent rules, even uncomfortable ones, produce more fairness in the long run than rules that bend for whoever is most powerful at the moment.

proverbs.translation: Ignorance of the law excuses no one.
proverbs.meaning: If rules can be dodged by claiming you did not know them, they stop being rules. Everyone is expected to know what the agreed standards are.

paragraphs: Rome's greatest gift to the modern world may not be its roads or its aqueducts — it is its law. Roman lawyers built a body of legal thinking so carefully argued and so practically tested that it still forms the foundation of legal systems across Europe and beyond. At the heart of it lies a solemn three-word mandate: pacta sunt servanda — agreements must be kept.

This is the engine of trust. When two people make a deal, sign a contract, or give their word, the entire value of that exchange rests on the convention that both will honour it. The Romans understood that trade across a vast empire was only possible if every merchant, governor and craftsman could rely on agreements being kept — by lawful enforcement if not by goodwill alone.

The law is harsh, but it is the law. A system that bends whenever someone powerful asks it to is not a system — it is just the authority of whoever happens to be in charge today. The inscription on every legal document was meant to carry the same weight whether you were a senator or an ordinary citizen. The compel to apply the same standard to everyone is what makes any legal system worth having.

Ignorance of the law excuses no one. If a rule can be avoided by claiming you were unaware of it, it only applies to the unlucky or the weak. For a sufficient legal system to work, everyone must acknowledge the same rules. No rebuke can be avoided simply by shrugging.

For children, these principles live in everyday promises. A rite as simple as 'I said I would do this, so I will' is the smallest version of pacta sunt servanda. The person who keeps their word consistently — even when it is inconvenient — builds a kind of trust that is extremely hard to buy back once lost.

collections.title: Who Stands to Gain?
collections.blurb: Cicero's forensic question and two Roman commercial principles that form one of the most useful critical-thinking toolkits ever assembled.

proverbs.translation: Who benefits?
proverbs.meaning: Cicero's forensic question, attributed to the judge Cassius. Before accepting any explanation for an event, ask who gains from it being believed.

proverbs.translation: Let the buyer beware.
proverbs.meaning: In any transaction, the buyer is responsible for assessing what they are actually getting. Nobody is required to advertise their own flaws.

proverbs.translation: Something for something.
proverbs.meaning: Most real-world exchanges have a return expectation — visible or hidden. Understand what someone expects before you accept what they offer.

paragraphs: The Roman lawyer Cicero credited a judge named Cassius with a question that has guided investigators, detectives and careful thinkers ever since: Cui bono? — Who benefits? When something happens, when a story is told, when a deal is offered, the first thing to ask is who gains from it. The answer rarely explains everything, but it almost always tells you where to start looking.

Scientists use a version of this when checking their own hypothesis: am I drawn to this conclusion because the evidence points here, or because I want it to be true? That is the subjective, internal version of cui bono — the self-interest we carry inside our own perception. Bias is not always imposed from outside. Sometimes we build it ourselves.

Let the buyer beware. This is sometimes used to excuse dishonest sellers, but its original meaning was about the buyer's responsibility. In the markets of ancient Rome, merchants were not required to allege every fault in what they sold. The cautious buyer was expected to scrutinise the goods themselves, rather than relying on the seller's enthusiasm. Check before you commit.

Something for something — quid pro quo — is the most honest description of how most transactions work. When someone offers something valuable without explanation, the clear-eyed question is: what do they expect in return? This is not a suspicious way of thinking. It is a realistic one. Understanding the full shape of an exchange prevents surprises.

Together, these three Roman questions form a practical toolkit: Who gains? What am I actually receiving? What does the other person expect? A child who learns to ask these questions calmly — without becoming cynical — will make far fewer decisions they later regret. Critical thinking is not suspicion. It is conviction backed by clear perception.

collections.title: Bread and Circuses
collections.blurb: Juvenal's sharp warning about distraction, divided attention and the oldest trick in the book for keeping people from thinking too hard.

proverbs.translation: Bread and circuses.
proverbs.meaning: Juvenal's two-word diagnosis of how power keeps people passive: provide enough food and entertainment, and most people will not ask difficult questions about who is in charge. (Juvenal, Satires)

proverbs.translation: Divide and rule.
proverbs.meaning: The oldest principle for managing a large group: keep people focused on their differences from each other, and they rarely organise against those at the top.

proverbs.translation: The world wants to be deceived, therefore let it be deceived.
proverbs.meaning: A harsh Roman observation: many people actively prefer comfortable stories to difficult truths. Recognising this in yourself is the first step to avoiding it.

paragraphs: The Roman poet Juvenal was not gentle about his fellow citizens. Writing around 100 AD, he watched the crowds pack into the Colosseum and collect free grain, and reached a blunt conclusion: the people had traded their collective voice for comfort. They no longer seriously debated who should hold power. All they wanted was enough bread and a circus to watch. Panem et circenses — bread and circuses — became his shorthand for a population that had chosen passive entertainment over active citizenship.

Juvenal was not inventing a conspiracy. He was describing a mechanism that Roman rulers understood very well, which is why they funded enormous entertainment venues and kept the grain provision running even under financial pressure. Allocate enough food and spectacle, and the hierarchy below stays stable. The regime does not need to compel obedience when people are well-fed and occupied.

Divide and rule is the companion principle. A regime that wants stability finds it useful to keep different groups within a population focused on their differences — by region, by tradition, by grievance. If people are fighting with each other, they rarely organise to ask why those further up the hierarchy have so much more. This is not a theory about ancient Rome alone — it is a pattern that has repeated across many centuries.

The world wants to be deceived. Many people, given the choice, select the comfortable version of a story over the accurate one. They accept the easy explanation. They ignore the convention-breaking fact. Those who notice this tendency in themselves — and actively work against it — are much harder to mislead.

For children today, these proverbs are essential media literacy. When something is offered free and immediately entertaining, the useful question is: what does the person offering this get from my attention? When two groups are fighting fiercely, ask whether both sides benefit from that fight or whether someone above them does. Being curious and alert — rather than passive and entertained — is what it means to think for yourself.

collections.title: If Not Now, When?
collections.blurb: Three questions Rabbi Hillel asked two thousand years ago — and the Jewish tradition has been answering them ever since.

proverbs.native: אם אין אני לי, מי לי?
proverbs.romanisation: Im ein ani li, mi li?
proverbs.translation: If I am not for myself, who will be for me?
proverbs.meaning: The first of Hillel the Elder's three questions in Pirkei Avot. Nobody else can stand up for your life on your behalf.

proverbs.translation: And when I am only for myself, what am I?
proverbs.meaning: Hillel's second question. A life that helps no one else is hardly a life at all.

proverbs.translation: And if not now, when?
proverbs.meaning: Hillel's sharpest line. Tomorrow keeps moving further away the longer we wait.

paragraphs: Two thousand years ago, a Jewish teacher named Hillel was asked to summarise his faith. He answered with three short questions that have echoed down the centuries: 'If I am not for myself, who will be? If I am only for myself, what am I? And if not now, when?' Together they have become almost a mantra of Jewish life.

The first question reminds us that nobody else can live our life for us. A child must learn to stand up for their own ideas, to look after their own health, to be poised and ready to speak. To furnish the world with your gifts, you first have to believe you have any.

The second question turns the camera around. If we look after only ourselves, we shrink. A good life is one where we are also nimble enough to notice the neighbour who needs help, the new pupil sitting alone, the bustling family on the bus who could use a smile.

And the third question is the sharpest. 'If not now, when?' How easy it is to let kindness lapse, to wait for a better moment that never comes, to stand on the verge of doing the right thing and then find an excuse. Hillel's answer is gentle but firm: tomorrow is not a lawful currency.

Around the Sabbath table, these questions still live on. They are not a heavy rite but a sprightly little nudge — be brave, be kind, and be quick. The minute we have right now is the only minute we ever truly own.

collections.title: A Golden Key Opens Every Door
collections.blurb: Hard-won Yiddish proverbs on money, resources and the honest truth about what opens doors and what closes them.

proverbs.native: אַ גאָלדענער שליסל עפנט אַלע טירן
proverbs.romanisation: A goldener shlissel efnt ale tirn
proverbs.translation: A golden key opens every door.
proverbs.meaning: Money is not everything, but pretending it counts for nothing is also untrue. Financial resources give you choices that stay closed without them.

proverbs.native: אַז מען האָט געלט, איז מען קלוג, שיין, און מען קען זינגען
proverbs.romanisation: Az men hot gelt, iz men klug, sheyn, un men ken zingen
proverbs.translation: When you have money, you are considered wise, good-looking, and can sing well too.
proverbs.meaning: A sharp observation about human nature: the same person is treated very differently depending on whether they appear to have resources.

proverbs.native: געלט איז אַ זאַלב צו אַלע וואונדן
proverbs.romanisation: Gelt iz a zalb tsu ale vundn
proverbs.translation: Money is a salve for all wounds.
proverbs.meaning: Resources ease almost every practical difficulty. This is not a moral claim — it is an observation about how the world actually works.

paragraphs: Jewish communities in Eastern Europe lived for centuries under laws that restricted what work they could do, what land they could own, and where they could settle. In that hard environment, they developed a clear-eyed view of money — not because they were greedy, but because they had seen plainly what life without any looked like. From those centuries of thrifty resourcefulness came some of the world's most honest proverbs about wealth.

A golden key opens every door. This is not a command to be lavish or opulent. It is a simple statement of what the community had observed again and again: money gives you choices. Without it, doors stay shut — to schools, to travel, to starting a business, to protecting your family when needed. With even a modest provision, a family could negotiate, plan, and act instead of simply endure.

A second saying cuts sharper: when you have money, people consider you wise, good-looking, and talented. This one smiles as it stings. The same person with empty pockets is often dismissed; the same person with resources is suddenly listened to. Pretending this dynamic does not exist leaves you unprepared for it.

The Yiddish tradition was not about accumulating wealth until you were opulent while others lived a sparse existence. The goal was sufficient provision — enough to be free, enough to keep learning, enough to compensate others fairly and allocate resources wisely. The livelihood worth having was one that brought dignity without requiring you to exploit others to furnish it.

For a child today, these proverbs are not an invitation to be materialistic. They are an invitation to be realistic. Understanding how resources work — how money flows, what it opens, what it cannot buy — is not cynical. It is the literacy of the real world. The person who understands money is far harder to take advantage of than the person who has decided it does not matter.

collections.title: Man Plans, God Laughs
collections.blurb: Yiddish wisdom on the gap between our best-laid plans and what life actually delivers — and what to do when they diverge.

proverbs.translation: Man plans, God laughs.
proverbs.meaning: Life rarely cooperates with even our most careful plans. Build them anyway — but hold them loosely.

proverbs.native: אַז מע קען נישט אַריבער, גייט מען אַרונטער
proverbs.romanisation: Az me ken nisht ariber, geyt men arunter
proverbs.translation: If you can't go over, go under.
proverbs.meaning: When the direct route is blocked, find another way. There is almost always another way — if you refuse to accept the first obstacle as the final answer.

proverbs.native: קיין עצה איז נישט אַזוי גוט ווי אַ גוטע חרטה
proverbs.romanisation: Keyn eytse iz nisht azoy gut vi a gute kharate
proverbs.translation: No advice is as good as a good regret.
proverbs.meaning: A genuine lesson learned from a real mistake is often more useful than any advice given in advance.

paragraphs: Three Yiddish words say it plainly: mentsh tracht, Got lacht — man plans, God laughs. It became one of the most widely known sayings in the Eastern European Jewish world because it captured something everyone had experienced. You plan carefully, prepare sensibly, set everything in order. Then life lurched sideways anyway — a volatile change of circumstances, an unexpected obstacle — and the whole resolution collapses.

This is not a reason to stop planning. The same culture that coined man plans also said: if you can't go over, go under. When the direct path is blocked, a practical person finds the route around, beneath, or through. The substitute path may be longer and messier, but it still leads somewhere useful. Accepting the first obstacle as a final answer is a choice, not a fact.

The lesson is about the difference between a plan and a grip. A plan is valuable: it tells you where to start and what to bring. But holding a plan so rigidly that you cannot adapt when things shift is a precarious way of thinking. The person who can acknowledge that circumstances have changed, and pivot without panic, survives situations that defeat those who cannot let go.

Regret also has its place. A third Yiddish saying observes that sometimes a good regret is the best advice of all. This is not self-punishment. It is the honest acknowledgement of what went wrong, used quickly and practically to rectify the next attempt. Look back for exactly as long as it takes to learn something useful — then keep walking.

Children who grow up hearing man plans, God laughs are not being taught to give up. They are being taught that flexibility and the willingness to try a different convention when the first one fails are not signs of weakness. They are the most important skills any planner can have.

collections.title: A Word is Like a Shot Arrow
collections.blurb: Yiddish proverbs on the dangerous power of words — and why knowing when to stay quiet is a harder skill than knowing what to say.

proverbs.native: אַ וואָרט איז ווי אַ פֿײַל: אַז עס שיסט אַרויס, קען מען עס נישט צוריקברענגען
proverbs.romanisation: A vort iz vi a fayl: az es shist aroys, ken men es nisht tsurikbrengen
proverbs.translation: A word is like an arrow: once it flies out, you cannot bring it back.
proverbs.meaning: Spoken words cannot be unspoken. Think before you fire.

proverbs.native: אַ לאַנגע צונג איז אַן אָרעמקייט
proverbs.romanisation: A lange tsung iz an oremkeyt
proverbs.translation: A long tongue is a poverty.
proverbs.meaning: The person who talks too much reveals their own plans, mistakes and weaknesses before they need to.

proverbs.native: שווייגן איז גאָלד, רעדן איז זילבער
proverbs.romanisation: Shvaygn iz gold, redn iz zilber
proverbs.translation: Silence is gold, speech is silver.
proverbs.meaning: Knowing when to stay quiet is a harder and rarer skill than knowing what to say.

paragraphs: One of the sharpest Yiddish proverbs compares a spoken word to an arrow. Once the bowstring is released, the arrow is already flying — and no amount of lament, apology or explanation can bring it back. The Eastern European Jewish communities who preserved this saying had seen it prove true many times: a careless remark to the wrong person, a heated word in a crowded market, could cause damage that lasted years.

This culture of cautious speech was not timidity. These were people who argued passionately, debated every word of sacred texts, and spoke out about injustice at real personal risk. The lesson was not silence as cowardice — it was silence as discipline. The cautious speaker thinks first. The hesitant speaker waits for the right moment. A sparse, well-chosen remark lands harder than a torrent of noise.

A long tongue is a poverty. The person who bellows everything they think turns their words into a liability. They reveal plans too early. They fire back with inflammatory remarks when a subtle answer would have done far more. They describe their suspicions before checking whether they are accurate. Every unnecessary word is information given away for free.

Silence is gold, speech is silver. This does not mean silence is always right. Sometimes speaking out is the braver and more necessary thing to do. But the person who defaults to talking — who mutters into every gap and fills every moment with noise — rarely earns the weight that a carefully chosen word can carry. A rebuke that is quiet and precise lands harder than one that is shouted.

In a world where messages now fly out in seconds and can almost never be fully deleted, these old Yiddish arrows are sharper than ever. Think before you send. An inflammatory comment, a suspicious mood expressed without checking, a rushed reply — all travel at arrow speed. The string cannot be un-pulled once released.

collections.title: Better Bread and Onion with Joy
collections.blurb: Yiddish proverbs on contentment, scarcity and what actually keeps a person going when the circumstances around them are difficult.

proverbs.native: בעסער ברויט מיט ציבעלע אין פֿריילעכקייט, איידער בראָטן מיט טרויעריקייט
proverbs.romanisation: Beser broyt mit tsibele in freylekheyt, eyder brotn mit troyer
proverbs.translation: Better bread with onion and joy than roast meat with sorrow.
proverbs.meaning: The quality of what you eat matters far less than the quality of how you feel while eating it. Warmth and honest company are real ingredients.

proverbs.native: עס איז נישט אַזוי גוט מיט געלט, ווי עס איז שלעכט אָן דעם
proverbs.romanisation: Es iz nisht azoy gut mit gelt, vi es iz shlekht on dem
proverbs.translation: It is not as good with money as it is bad without it.
proverbs.meaning: Having money does not guarantee happiness. But the absence of money is a real, daily difficulty that should not be romanticised.

proverbs.translation: Difficulties strengthen people.
proverbs.meaning: Hard times build a kind of person that comfortable times cannot. This is not a comforting promise — it is an observation from experience.

paragraphs: Jewish communities in Eastern Europe knew poverty not as an idea but as a daily fact. Winters were bleak, resources were sparse, and laws often prevented families from earning a comfortable living. Out of that experience came a set of proverbs about survival, contentment and what actually keeps a person going when conditions around them are miserable.

Better bread with onion and joy than roast meat with sorrow. This is not romantic simplicity. It is a practical observation: food does not taste good when the atmosphere is wretched. A hearty loaf shared with people you trust beats a lavish dinner eaten in fear or silence. The warmth that keeps a family stout through hard times is more valuable than any luxury — and unlike luxuries, it cannot be taken away by law or circumstance.

The companion proverb is equally clear-eyed: it is not as good with money as it is bad without it. Notice the careful phrasing. It does not claim money is everything. It acknowledges that having it is not magical — but missing it is genuinely grim. A thrifty, resolute family can manage without wealth. A destitute one cannot. This is not pessimism — it is precision.

None of this is sugar-coating. Life without sufficient resources is genuinely hard, and these proverbs do not pretend otherwise. What they offer is this: within whatever you have, find the sufficient amount of warmth, of honest company, of shared effort, that makes each day bearable and sometimes even good. The joy alongside the bread and onion is real, even when it is small.

Difficulties strengthen people. The Yiddish communities that produced this saying had endured more than most. They were not being contented about suffering — they were recording what they had observed: that people who pass through real hardship and come out the other side clear-eyed and resolute have something that ease alone cannot build. Understanding that this is possible is itself a form of preparation.

collections.title: When You Need a Thief, Take Him Off the Gallows
collections.blurb: Yiddish proverbs on human nature — unsentimental, observant and honest about how people actually behave when circumstances change.

proverbs.native: אַז מע דאַרף דעם גנבֿ, נעמט מען אים אַראָפ פֿון דער תּליה
proverbs.romanisation: Az me darf dem ganev, nemt men im arop fun der tlie
proverbs.translation: When you need a thief, you take him down from the gallows.
proverbs.meaning: Categories are not permanent. Someone considered suspicious or untrustworthy may become the most useful person available when the situation changes.

proverbs.native: אַז דער גנבֿ האָט קיין הזדמנות נישט, איז ער אַן עהרלעכער מענטש
proverbs.romanisation: Az der ganev hot keyn hazdomne nisht, iz er an erlekher mentsh
proverbs.translation: When a thief has no opportunity, he is an honest man.
proverbs.meaning: Many people behave well simply because conditions make it easy. Integrity under pressure — when breaking a rule would actually benefit you — is the real test.

proverbs.native: וואָס וועט נישט גיין פֿאַר געלט, וועט גיין פֿאַר מערער געלט
proverbs.romanisation: Vos vet nisht geyn far gelt, vet geyn far merer gelt
proverbs.translation: What will not be done for money will be done for more money.
proverbs.meaning: An honest observation about motivation: most stated resistance is not purely moral — it is about the price not yet being high enough.

paragraphs: Perhaps the sharpest category of Yiddish wisdom is its sayings about human nature — unsentimental, precise and sometimes darkly funny. These proverbs were not written by people who assumed the best about everyone. They were produced by communities that had learned, over many hard centuries, that seeing people clearly was a survival skill.

When you need a thief, take him down from the gallows. This sounds notorious — even scandalous. But the meaning is practical: categories are not permanent. A person considered evasive, stealthy or illicit in one situation may be the most capable person available in another. A bias that dismisses someone based solely on their reputation rather than their actual usefulness is the kind of bias that gets people into trouble when circumstances change.

When a thief has no opportunity, he is an honest man. This proverb does not excuse dishonesty. It is an observation. Many people who allege high principles have simply never been placed in a situation where breaking a rule would benefit them significantly. Scrutinise the integrity, not just the claim. A person who is honest because they have nothing to gain from dishonesty is not the same as one who is honest when something real is at stake.

What will not be done for money will be done for more money. This is the bluntest of the three. It does not say that everyone can be compelled. It says that most resistance has a price, and that a clear-eyed person acknowledges this rather than pretending all refusals are purely principled. Understanding what people are actually responding to — not just what they say they are responding to — is not cynical. It is precise.

These are not sayings to live by in a mean-spirited way. You can discriminate carefully between who to trust and still treat everyone with basic dignity. Seeing people clearly is not the same as treating them badly — it is the first step to dealing with them honestly.

collections.title: Tie Your Camel
collections.blurb: A famous Hadith on faith and practical action — and the way one short Arabic sentence travelled the world.

proverbs.translation: Tie your camel, and trust in God.
proverbs.meaning: From a famous Hadith. Faith was never meant to replace common sense — pray with one hand, and work with the other.

proverbs.translation: Deeds are judged by intentions.
proverbs.meaning: Another famous Hadith. What matters is not just the outward act, but the heart that performs it.

paragraphs: In the deserts of old Arabia, a man once said to the Prophet Muhammad, 'Should I leave my camel untied and trust in God?' The Prophet, peace be upon him, replied with a line that has lived ever since: 'Tie your camel — and then trust in God.' Faith was never meant to replace common sense.

The saying is one of the most notorious examples of Arabic wisdom because it captures so much in so few words. To encapsulate centuries of teaching, it says simply: pray with one hand and work with the other. Heaven helps the rider who has first tightened her own saddle.

In daily life this principle is everywhere. A pupil who only hopes to pass an exam, without revising, has left the camel loose. A traveller who refuses to take a coat into the mountains, certain that the weather will obey his prayers, may soon discover that prayer and preparation are partners, not rivals.

The lesson is gentle but clear. Trust is not a drastic abandoning of effort; it is the calm that comes after you have done your part. The wise Arabic teachers refused to discriminate between the spiritual and the practical. To them, washing your hands, mending your shoes and saying your prayers were all stitched into the same fabric.

Sayings like this travelled across the Muslim world along trade routes, where they would disperse through cities from Cordoba to Samarkand. A traveller would carry the words like a coin in a pouch, sharing them at one caravan stop after another, until the same little proverb circulated as widely as the silk on the merchants' backs.

To us today, 'tie your camel' may denote nothing more than a piece of practical advice. Yet for centuries of Arab travellers, who clambered onto their camels each dawn and rode for weeks across the sand, those four words were a quiet contract with the world. The proverb's own saga is itself remarkable: it began as a quick remark in a desert oasis, meandered through generations of teachers and storytellers, and ended up quoted in cafés and classrooms from Morocco to Indonesia.

collections.title: Ink of the Scholar
collections.blurb: Sayings from the great age of Islamic scholarship, when Baghdad's House of Wisdom translated the world.

proverbs.native: اطلبوا العلم من المهد إلى اللحد.
proverbs.romanisation: Uṭlubū al-'ilma min al-mahdi ilā al-laḥd.
proverbs.translation: Seek knowledge from the cradle to the grave.
proverbs.meaning: A famous saying urging lifelong learning — no one is too young to start, and no one is too old to continue.

proverbs.native: مداد العلماء أفضل من دماء الشهداء.
proverbs.romanisation: Midād al-'ulamā' afḍal min dimā' ash-shuhadā'.
proverbs.translation: The ink of the scholars is more precious than the blood of the martyrs.
proverbs.meaning: An old saying long quoted in the Muslim world: the patient work of study is one of the highest of all callings.

proverbs.translation: Knowledge is light.
proverbs.meaning: A short Arabic proverb. To learn is to push back the darkness, both around you and inside you.

paragraphs: In the 9th century, Baghdad was the most exciting city on earth. Its caliphs had founded a House of Wisdom — bayt al-hikma — where Arabic, Greek, Persian and Indian scholars worked side by side translating, copying and arguing. From this glowing vault of books came medicine, astronomy, algebra and much more.

One old saying still quoted today is this: 'The ink of the scholar is more precious than the blood of the martyr.' For Islamic civilisation this was not just poetry. To read a single excerpt from Aristotle, to study one specimen of a healing plant, to copy out an old astronomical chart — these acts mattered as much as any battle.

Mathematics flourished. The very word algebra comes from al-jabr, written in Baghdad in around 820. Astronomers measured the spatial layout of the stars and built precise instruments. Scholars created a kind of matrix of human knowledge in which every discovery was carefully cross-referenced with the next.

Linguists were equally rigorous. Arab grammarians studied every inflection of every verb, every hieroglyph-like marking on the page, every small change in meaning. Books on a single letter could run to hundreds of pages. A scholar who tried to plagiarise another's work was shamed for life.

In the great markets of old Damascus, Cairo and Granada you could buy a book on almost any subject — a herbal census of Mediterranean plants, a guide to Indian numerals, an excerpt of Persian poetry. Each volume was a quiet artefact of an old, generous belief: that to learn together is one of the holiest things humans can do.

collections.title: Trust, but Verify
collections.blurb: Two old peasant proverbs that, between them, summarise Russia's quiet, patient relationship with the world.

proverbs.translation: Trust, but verify.
proverbs.meaning: An old Russian peasant proverb made famous worldwide by Ronald Reagan during nuclear talks with the Soviet Union.

proverbs.translation: The quieter you drive, the further you will go.
proverbs.meaning: A Russian way of saying both 'slow and steady wins the race' and 'haste makes waste.'

paragraphs: During the long Cold War years, an old Russian saying suddenly appeared on the lips of world leaders. Doveryai, no proveryai — 'Trust, but verify.' President Reagan used it again and again in his nuclear talks with Soviet leaders, and Mikhail Gorbachev half-jokingly complained that he heard it at every meeting.

The proverb is older than any treaty. Russian peasants used it for centuries when buying a horse, hiring a worker or sending a child off with a friend. Russia is a vast country, full of long roads and long winters. A wise person trusts — but then, gently, also checks. Even the most trustworthy harness has lurched loose on a bumpy track.

In a tense world of espionage and deterrent threats, that quiet peasant proverb gave both sides a workable rule. We will believe your promise to destroy these missiles, the Americans said, but we will also send our inspectors. Not because we are suspicious of you in particular, but because trust without checking can become foolishness.

Russians have another saying that fits beside this one: tishe yedesh, dal'she budesh — 'The quieter you go, the further you will reach.' Hurrying through life on a stealthy shortcut may seem clever, but evasive answers, uneasy compromises and clandestine deals usually come back to bite. The slow honest road, the proverb hints, is also the safest.

Even today, when relations grow inflammatory and headlines turn shrill, a quiet voice in Russian kitchens still mutters: doveryai, no proveryai. Trust people — most of them deserve it. But keep your eyes open all the same.

collections.title: Measure Seven Times
collections.blurb: The Russian carpenter's golden rule, and the long, patient view of work that built villages to last centuries.

proverbs.native: Семь раз отмерь, один раз отрежь.
proverbs.romanisation: Sem' raz otmer', odin raz otrezh'.
proverbs.translation: Measure seven times, cut once.
proverbs.meaning: The Russian carpenter's golden rule. Plan carefully — a mistake in cutting wood, or in choosing a path in life, is hard to undo.

proverbs.translation: Patience and work grind through everything.
proverbs.meaning: A favourite Russian proverb. There is no obstacle so vast that calm, steady effort cannot wear it down over time.

paragraphs: There are few sayings more practical than the Russian carpenter's rule: sem' raz otmer', odin raz otrezh' — 'Measure seven times, cut once.' Behind it lies a country full of long winters, where a single mistake in building a fence or a roof could cost a family dearly. Get it right at the start, the proverb says, or pay for it later.

Russian craftsmen took this to heart. A village technician who built sledges or coffin chests would measure his planks again and again before laying a saw against the wood. He had no money to waste on a hack job. The amateur cuts first and apologises later; the master measures slowly and apologises never.

Walk through an old Russian village even today and you can see the result. Houses with their gnarled wooden beams stand for two hundred years, their carved windows still neat. A dilapidated barn is rare; a rickety roof rarer still. The lesson is that a careful first plan is worth more than a stupendous finished mistake.

Russians have a second proverb that pairs perfectly with the first: terpenie i trud vse peretrut — 'Patience and work grind through everything.' The most towering mountain is moved one barrow-load at a time. The supple wood becomes a fine sledge only after weeks of careful steaming. There is no real substitute for slow effort.

Children growing up in Russian families often hear both sayings together, on rainy afternoons when a long task feels impossible. Measure seven times. And then keep going, patiently, until the work is done. There is something deeply hopeful in that pair of small, calm sentences.

collections.title: Praise No Day Till Evening
collections.blurb: Hávamál sayings on the danger of judging too early, the wisdom of checking before you enter, and why being middling-wise beats thinking you already know everything.

proverbs.native: At kveldi skal dag leyfa; mæki, er reyndr er; ís, er yfir kemr
proverbs.romanisation: At kveldi skal dag leyfa; mæki, er reyndr er; ís, er yfir kemr
proverbs.translation: Praise the day at evening; the sword when it has been tested; the ice when you have crossed it.
proverbs.meaning: Judge nothing until it has been fully proved. A plan that looks perfect at dawn can be ruins by dusk. (Hávamál, stanza 81)

proverbs.native: Gáttir allar, áðr gangi fram, um skoðask skyli, um skyggnask skyli
proverbs.romanisation: Gáttir allar, áðr gangi fram, um skoðask skyli, um skyggnask skyli
proverbs.translation: At every doorway before going in, look around carefully, peer in all directions.
proverbs.meaning: Slow down before entering any new situation. The wary person who scans the room first survives where the rushing person does not. (Hávamál, stanza 1)

proverbs.native: Meðalsnotr skyli manna hverr, æva til snotr sé
proverbs.romanisation: Meðalsnotr skyli manna hverr, æva til snotr sé
proverbs.translation: Middling wise should every person be; never too wise.
proverbs.meaning: The person who thinks they have figured out everything has already stopped paying attention — and that is when danger walks in. (Hávamál, stanza 56)

paragraphs: The Vikings were not a sentimental people. They crossed icy seas in open boats and spent winters in places that wanted to kill them. Their most famous book of wisdom, the Hávamál — the Sayings of the High One — opens not with poetry about glory but with a single cautious instruction: before you walk through any door, look around carefully. Check the benches. Notice who is already seated. The wary guest survives; the rushing one does not.

Praise the day at evening; praise the sword when it has been tested; praise the ice when you have crossed it. This stout list is a precise warning against celebrating too early. A sword looks impressive in the forge but may shatter in battle. Ice looks solid from the bank but may crack underfoot. The plan that seemed perfect at noon can fall apart by sundown. The Norseman who praised things only after they had proved themselves was the one who was never caught by surprise.

The Hávamál was deeply suspicious of people who seemed too sure of themselves. Be middling wise, it counsels — never too wise. A person who thinks they already understand everything has closed themselves to new information. They walk into rooms without looking. They ignore subtle signs. Their own bias towards cleverness becomes the very trap that catches them.

This is not an instruction to be hesitant forever. The Norse expected people to act decisively — but only after scanning the situation with vigilant, clear eyes. Scrutinise first. Acknowledge what you actually see, without the distortion of what you hoped or feared to find. Then move.

To this day, good detectives, good doctors and good coaches think this way. They warily resist deciding what something means before they have gathered enough information. They check suspicious details. They pause at doorways. The day is to be praised at evening — not a moment sooner.

collections.title: A Friend Tested Twice
collections.blurb: Hávamál's unsentimental guide to friendship — not as a feeling, but as a practice proven under pressure.

proverbs.native: Vin sínum skal maðr vinr vera, þeim ok þess vin
proverbs.romanisation: Vin sínum skal maðr vinr vera, þeim ok þess vin
proverbs.translation: To your friend be a friend, to them and to their friend.
proverbs.meaning: Friendship is not just a feeling between two people — it is a network of mutual responsibility. (Hávamál, stanza 42)

proverbs.native: En óvinar síns skyli engi maðr vinar vinr vera
proverbs.romanisation: En óvinar síns skyli engi maðr vinar vinr vera
proverbs.translation: No one should be a friend to the enemy of their friend.
proverbs.meaning: Loyalty has a shape. A friend who is also comfortable with your enemies is not fully a friend. (Hávamál, stanza 42)

proverbs.translation: Man is the joy of man.
proverbs.meaning: We genuinely need each other — not only for warmth, but for the practical work of surviving and building anything worth having. (Hávamál, stanza 47)

paragraphs: The Norse had a very clear idea of what a friend actually was — and it was less amiable than many people expect. In the Hávamál, Odin teaches that a friend is not someone who smiles at you and says kind things in public. A friend is someone who has been tested: who showed up when things were difficult, kept their word when breaking it would have been easier, and proved themselves stout when you needed them most.

To your friend, be a friend. This sounds simple, but the line does not stop there. It continues: also be a friend to your friend's friend. Friendship, in the Norse world, was a network of hearty, mutual duty — not just a sociable feeling between two people. If you genuinely care for someone, you also take seriously the people they depend on.

The companion teaching is equally direct: no one should be a friend to the enemy of their friend. The Norse were reluctant to tolerate grey areas in loyalty. They expected people to discriminate clearly — this person is with me, that one is not. A friend who is also tolerant and comfortable around those who have wronged you is sending a signal worth acknowledging. Not every signal needs immediate action. But they should all be noticed.

None of this means the Vikings were cold or calculating. Man is the joy of man, the Hávamál says simply — we need each other. The conviction behind this is genuine: without real friends, a long winter, a difficult journey or a dark year is not sufficient. People need people. But they need true ones, not just pleasant-seeming ones.

For children, the practical lesson is this: not everyone who is pleasant to you is a friend. Wait. See what happens when something goes wrong. Does this person stay? Do they keep their word? The friend worth keeping is the one who shows up twice — once when things are easy, and once when they are not.

collections.title: Cattle Die, Kindred Die
collections.blurb: The most famous stanza of the Hávamál on what is mortal, what is not, and what actually survives long after everything else is gone.

proverbs.native: Deyr fé, deyja frændr, deyr sjálfr it sama
proverbs.romanisation: Deyr fé, deyja frændr, deyr sjálfr it sama
proverbs.translation: Cattle die, kinsmen die, you yourself die.
proverbs.meaning: Nothing you own or love is permanent. Pretending otherwise is the deepest foolishness. (Hávamál, stanza 76)

proverbs.native: En orðstírr deyr aldregi hveim er sér góðan getr
proverbs.romanisation: En orðstírr deyr aldregi hveim er sér góðan getr
proverbs.translation: But a good reputation never dies, for those who earn one.
proverbs.meaning: After everything else is gone, what remains is the story other people tell about you. (Hávamál, stanza 76–77)

proverbs.native: Ek veit einn, at aldri deyr: dómr um dauðan hvern
proverbs.romanisation: Ek veit einn, at aldri deyr: dómr um dauðan hvern
proverbs.translation: I know one thing that never dies: the judgment passed on each dead person.
proverbs.meaning: When you are gone, people will talk. What they say depends entirely on what you actually did. (Hávamál, stanza 77)

paragraphs: The most famous stanza in all of Norse literature is also its most direct. Cattle die, it says. Kinsmen die. You die yourself. No softening. No comfort. The animals you owned are gone.
