The family you loved is gone. In the end, so are you. This is not a threat — it is a solemn fact that the Norse treated every person as mature enough to hear.

But the poem does not stop there. One thing I know that never dies: the judgment passed on each dead person. Your earthly goods are finite. Your body is mortal, however glorious it feels today. But your reputation — the story your neighbours, your enemies and your children tell about you — is the only legacy that outlasts everything else.

This is practical as well as stirring. The Vikings built memorials and raised runestones not to glorify the dead but to record what they actually did. The inscription on a stone might say a man was trustworthy, a woman worked hard, a ship was well sailed. These are tribute stones of deeds, not flattery. The saga kept only what was real.

An immortal name, in the Norse sense, is not about being famous. It is about being someone other people can honestly point to and say: that person kept their word, did what was needed, and did not flinch. That is the only kind of fame that cannot be taken away.

For a child, this is not a lesson about death. It is a lesson about today. Every kind or unkind thing you do slowly builds the story that other people will carry about you. A reputation is half-written long before anyone realises it. The Norse advice is practical: write it carefully, starting now.

collections.title: Never Walk Without Your Weapon
collections.blurb: Hávamál on preparation, self-reliance and the most important tool any traveller can carry — which turns out not to be a sword.

proverbs.native: Vápnum sínum skala maðr velli á, feti ganga framar
proverbs.romanisation: Vápnum sínum skala maðr velli á, feti ganga framar
proverbs.translation: Never walk a step from your weapon on the open ground.
proverbs.meaning: Always be prepared for the situation to change. The person who left their tools at home is always the one who needed them. (Hávamál, stanza 38)

proverbs.native: Sá einn veit, er víða ratar, ok hefr fjölð um farit
proverbs.romanisation: Sá einn veit, er víða ratar, ok hefr fjölð um farit
proverbs.translation: He alone knows who has travelled widely and seen much of the world.
proverbs.meaning: Experience teaches things that advice never can. The world looks very different once you have actually been out in it. (Hávamál, stanza 18)

proverbs.native: Byrði betri berr-at maðr brautu at en sé mannvit mikit
proverbs.romanisation: Byrði betri berr-at maðr brautu at en sé mannvit mikit
proverbs.translation: No better burden can a man carry on the road than much common sense.
proverbs.meaning: Weapons matter — but the sharpest tool any traveller can carry is a prepared, watchful mind. (Hávamál, stanza 10)

paragraphs: Old Norse life was practical in a way that is hard to imagine today. You might need to cross a freezing fjord, walk several days through hostile wilderness, or arrive at a settlement you had never visited. The Hávamál's instruction was direct: never walk a step from your weapon on open ground. Because no one can know in advance what comes next — and being unprepared in a perilous moment can be fatal.

This is not an invitation to be fearful. The Norse sent scouts ahead precisely because vigilant preparation is what allows the main group to move confidently. A resolute traveller does not look afraid — they look ready. The deterrent of obvious preparedness is itself a form of protection. Many threats never develop because the potential target is clearly not an easy one.

The proverb about carrying common sense is equally practical. Real weapons are perilous if you do not know when to use them. A robust mind — alert, adaptable, thinking ahead — is worth more than a sharp blade carried without care. The wilderness does not care whether you are brave. It only cares whether you came prepared.

Provision made before the journey matters too. The skilled Norse traveller checked their supplies before setting out: food, rope, a spare cloak, knowledge of the route. Anticipation was not nervousness — it was craft. The person who thought through what could go wrong and brought what was needed was the person who came home.

For life today, the weapon is preparation. For an exam, preparation is revision. For a match, preparation is practice. For a difficult conversation, preparation is thinking through what you want to say before you say it. The Norse lesson is not about fear — it is about keeping your tools sharp before you need them, not after.

collections.title: Better a Fool Silent
collections.blurb: Hávamál's blunt advice on when to speak, when to stay quiet, and why the person who says least often knows most.

proverbs.native: Ósnotr maðr, er með aldir kømr, þat er bazt, at hann þegi
proverbs.romanisation: Ósnotr maðr, er með aldir kømr, þat er bazt, at hann þegi
proverbs.translation: The unwise person, when they come among others, is best off staying silent.
proverbs.meaning: No one knows you know nothing — until you open your mouth and prove it. Silence preserves more options than speech. (Hávamál, stanza 27)

proverbs.native: Engi þat veit, at hann ekki kann, nema hann mæli til margt
proverbs.romanisation: Engi þat veit, at hann ekki kann, nema hann mæli til margt
proverbs.translation: No one knows a person knows nothing — unless they speak too much.
proverbs.meaning: Talking too much is the fastest way to reveal what you do not yet understand. (Hávamál, stanza 27)

proverbs.native: Meðalsnotr skyli manna hverr, æva til snotr sé
proverbs.romanisation: Meðalsnotr skyli manna hverr, æva til snotr sé
proverbs.translation: Middling wise should every person be; never too wise.
proverbs.meaning: The cleverest person in the room is not always the one doing the most talking — and rarely the one who thinks they know everything already. (Hávamál, stanza 56)

paragraphs: The Hávamál has no patience for people who talk too much. Its advice is delivered bluntly: the unwise person who comes among others is best off staying quiet. No one knows you know nothing, it continues — unless you speak too much. Silence, in the Norse world, was not weakness or being passive. It was a tactical position. It kept your knowledge and your ignorance equally concealed until you had a reason to reveal either.

The convention in many modern settings is the opposite: speak often, speak confidently, fill the silence. The Hávamál muttered its disagreement with this approach two thousand years ago. Bellowed opinions impress no one paying close attention. A sparse, well-chosen remark lands harder than a torrent of noise. The person who speaks rarely but accurately earns a solemn kind of weight that the constant talker never quite achieves.

None of this means saying nothing forever. The Norsemen were famously direct when the moment demanded it. The skill is knowing the difference. An inflammatory word at the wrong moment creates enemies unnecessarily. A rebuke delivered too loudly and too early signals that the speaker has lost control. A quiet, ominous stillness before speaking signals the opposite.

Middling wise should every person be; never too wise. The person who has decided they are the cleverest in the room has stopped learning. They are not actually listening to what is being said around them — they are waiting for their turn to prove they already knew. That gap in attention is exactly where they get caught.

The consensus of all three stanzas is this: talk less, observe more, and speak only when you have something to add that is worth the cost of revealing it. This is not a pleasant convention — it takes practice and some discomfort. But the person who masters it has a real advantage in almost every conversation they will ever have.
